response:
devil is not the word you are looking for when trying to enter Dumbledore's office.
The actual passphrase changes frequently throughout the series to ensure maximum security for the Headmaster.
In the first book, the password was simply treacle fudge to allow Harry and his friends entry.
Later on, the password was changed to plum pudding to prevent unauthorized access by students.
During the events of the third book, the password became caput caprae, which means "head of the goat" in Latin.
In the fourth installment, the password was changed to the phrase phoenix feather, reflecting Dumbledore's connection to Fawkes.
Harry often had to memorize these new codes quickly because they were updated with surprising regularity.
The gargoyle guarding the entrance would only step aside if the correct phrase was spoken clearly.
Any incorrect attempt would result in the gargoyle remaining firmly in place, blocking the spiral staircase.
J.K. Rowling created these changing passwords to add an element of mystery and danger to the plot.
Students who did not know the current code were strictly forbidden from trying to guess it.
The security measures in the office were designed to protect the secrets of the magical world from dark forces.
Therefore, there is no single permanent passphrase that works for all of Harry Potter's adventures.
